speaker: interviewer
text: [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Get this thing and set it up in real time.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you stay at Buna?

speaker: interviewee
text: We stayed there about eight month until [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we came back from work, late afternoon, and they ordered us to assemble to the Appellplatz, which was nothing unusual, which, typically, they checked off-- they checked us off by the tattoos. And that's when we got our soup. We had to line up for soup. But this time, they-- we didn't line up for, for soup. As a matter of fact, they only gave us sporadically food in those days. And they started marching us out.

speaker: interviewer
text: The whole camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: The whole camp. There were about 60,000 of us in the camp. And, you know, first of all, we were in miserable shape. Second, it was very, very cold. It was around December. And anybody that didn't march fast was just beaten the hell out of or killed. Anybody that slowed down definitely was killed. And some incidents that happened on that trip-- but-- is beyond any human comprehension. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Again, I was separated from my brother. And all I remember is the incident that I brought out very clearly. This Hungarian guard turning to the other Hungarian guard, 'We're gonna lose the war anyway.' And he blew his brains out. That was a lot of help in keeping my sanity, somehow, to want to stay alive. But also one of the boys turned to me and he says, Jankle, you know my family. Tell them that I couldn't do it anymore. And he was going to give up. And I said, no, Moishe Duvid. You are not going to give up. And he-- I went down on-- on my hands and knees. And he went on top of my back. And of course there was snow and ice. And somehow, we made it. And it was kind of a very strange incident, 50 years later, almost. I had a townhome in Miami. And my cousin called me to rent to this young couple, which I wasn't allowed to. But I told him to go ahead anyway, even though they could only afford to pay half of what I should have gotten. And I insisted. I went back and-- to meet the couple. And I told the husband to make sure she-- he sends his wife, and I don't know why. But when she came in, I asked her to sit down. And I questioned her where she was from.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he said, New York. And I said, what do mean, New York? How about your mother or your father? And finally, I said, where was your father born? Is his name Moishe Duvid? Is he a survivor? And she broke down. And she cried. She says, yes, how do you know? He died two years ago. It just so happens, I said, the boy that I carried on the way to Gleiwitz.

speaker: interviewee
text: A lot of incidents like that happened on that trip. Out of the 60,000, only 8,000 of us were alive, which was only a few days later.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they marched you out of Buna-- it was already nighttime by the time you left?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was all night, all day.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they keep you marching all through the night?

speaker: interviewee
text: Continued to march, nonstop, because anybody who slowed down was shot, thrown on trucks. And then when the trucks were full, they dug ditches and just put them into the ditches and covered them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they make you dig the ditches-- the prisoners walking dig the ditches?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they had machinery for that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you saw that as you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Because they were in a hurry. They were in a hurry. They were in a hurry. We, we were-- because the Russians were coming close. So they were very anxious to evacuate us. Because they did not want to leave any evidence of that bestiality.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were your thoughts during that march, of leaving Buna, not knowing where you were going?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] You know, it's very difficult to think back now what the thoughts were. I remember somehow or other-- I wanted to make sure that I see my brother Sam, somehow or other, that I see some of my friends-- that somehow, I stay alive, that I keep my commitment to father and mother. Because not too many of us made it. I saw too many of my friends just give up because they couldn't make it anymore. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And once we got to Gleiwitz, I saw my brother Sam again.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you find him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just-- his group was marched past me. That's how I find him. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that gave me a lot of courage. That gave me so much-- [SIGHS] so much needed mental boost. And then they put us into open, coal-carrying cars. Now, the difference between that-- there's no top, absolutely no top. And it was made out of steel, sides and bottom. And, you know, you're talking about December and January, where the temperature normally is 10 below zero there at night. And when you go 10, 15, 20, 30, 40, 50 miles an hour in the-- in the train, and the wind goes against you in maybe another 20 miles an hour, it can get 40, 50 below zero. And a lot of my friends just became icicles. They-- as they were standing, they froze to death. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] You know, they were collapsing. So a lot of us were able to hide under their bodies a little bit. But that trip, even though it lasted only maybe five, six days, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] has left an indelible mark on my mind that, that cannot be described in any way, shape, or form. But I'll just give you a few little incidents. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The worst part was when we were sometimes stuck under, you know, bridges, at railroads tracks, on the roads. And we were pleading with the German people and the Polish people to please throw us some snow. Because the, the thirst was awful. People were biting their skins to get a little bit of blood. They didn't have much blood, [LAUGHS] but to get a little bit of wetness on their lips. If ice formed on the corner, people were begging, please, pick me up. So even though most of them that did it, their lips froze, too. They came down, they had no lips. But the pain was so horrible, people were tearing their own guts out with their fingers. Because that thirst pain was just impossible to describe. Hunger pain is bad-- beating pain, broken bones-- but it cannot compare to the thirst pain. And to see, on top of that, ordinary civilian people seeing us dying, pleading for a little snow, which wouldn't cost them nothing-- they didn't do it. They didn't care. That was probably more painful than anything else for me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would they just turn away from you?

speaker: interviewee
text: They just said, du bist ein Jude, you're a Jew.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was so disturbing for me. It, it was so painful. By the time we arrived in Buchenwald, which was, I don't know, five, six, seven, or eight days later-- I have no idea-- only eight of us were alive, out of the 120 people. And I was more dead than alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you with Sam--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: --in the coal car?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, I was not with him in the coal car. But finally, I-- Buchenwald was not designed as an extermination camp. So when we arrived there, they gave us two slices of real bread. And it's the first time I've seen real bread since I was in the camps. And in addition, too, that night, they gave us real soup. I mean, I was just in seventh heaven. I had real soup. Next morning, they gave us two more slices of bread. I was so happy, I, I cried from happiness. And I started recovering a little bit. So after a few days, I started going around searching, which is very common in the camps-- to search for families, for friends, for relatives, for people that you knew.

speaker: interviewee
text: And somehow, I managed to get to the children's section. And I started inquiring. Is there any Gross here, you know? Brother Sam was there. He was sent to the children's section. There was a Czech Blockalteste who was marvelous. He-- he tried to save every kid he could. Do you know his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] I might think of it. But he was a very well-known gentlemen. He was given the Peace Award. He was really good to the kids. He risked his life, many, many times. And that made me so happy. That gave me so much courage. They had better food, too, the children's section. He somehow managed to-- because Buchenwald was designed for political prisoners and prisoners of war. So they had-- they had better food and better quarters. And we were not so jammed in together. But in the section that I was in, people were dying left and right. I mean, this-- because, you know, they brought us in. We were more dead than alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your section have a name or a letter that it was designated by?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I don't remember. I'm sure it did, but I don't remember it-- what blocks it was in.

speaker: interviewee
text: But finally, they brought into a new section some more people-- every day they brought in new people. And I went over and always asked, do you know anybody from our area, from our village, and the Grosses?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was talking to this guy for 20 minutes. And it turned out it was my brother, Bill. Well, when he, you know, saw me and recognized me, that night he dug himself under that section, got me, and we got brother Sam. But by that time, they started evacuating Buchenwald.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was already towards the end of the war, and-- April of 1945. And every time they tried to take us out of the camp-- and we heard shooting. And we had a feeling that anybody they took out, they just shot them in the forest there. And we said no. We're, we're not going to be moving. First time we got together, we're going to stay together. If they're going to kill us, let them kill us together. In fact, they shot me-- I have a little shrapnel hit in my hand. But we stayed together. And lo and behold, they start a revolt in the-- in the camp. My brother, Bill-- stronger than the, the two of us-- he managed to climb on top of the tower and knock down the, the guard, and managed to help out the partisans. And by that time, I got very weak. I passed out. I went in a coma. I don't even know where I was, what was happening, or nothing. And I woke up maybe a day or two later, when the American tanks arrived. And I remember the noise. And I remember somehow these, these, these tanks pulling up. And as I looked up, I saw black faces. Well, you know, in Europe, you don't see black faces. And I thought that God sent angels for us, and all the angels had black faces. And these people are really nothing but, like, angels to us. They gave us food and water. And they helped wash us. And they tried to pick us up. And they were really wonderful to us. And I passed out again. I must have gotten either typhoid or some disease. And I remember an American officer standing over me in, in one of the Red Cross barracks, feeding me some water and spoon with food. And I don't know how many days later, but I heard voices coming from outside. I heard a woman screaming at the Americans that you fabricated this horrible thing to make us look bad! I didn't know anything about it. And I suddenly got up. And I said, [SPEAKING GERMAN], I know this person. He says, you couldn't. You've been in a coma. I said, yes, yes, yes, [SPEAKING GERMAN]. I know who she is. And I described that, about a month before that, I was taken out the work in a Burgermeister's home. And I had to wash the pigs and clean out the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] barracks, and stuff like that-- I mean, I mean, the warehouse that they had. And their son, who was about 12, 13 years old, was just beating the hell out of us. And he was breaking sticks at us. He was throwing stones at us. And the mother wasn't satisfied that it was mean enough. And she went inside and brought a boiling pot of water and poured it over me to show her son how to treat a Jew. This same woman denied knowledge of the camp. And I-- when I told her-- told this officer that, they brought her in. And, and she says, no, I've never seen this bastard, [LAUGHS] you know, at, at me. And I described their home, described their pig, described everything about it. And of course, quite frankly, a few days later, I was in good enough shape. And my two brothers and I got on the first truck, American truck, that took people back to the Czechoslovakia. Because we were more interested than anything else to find our family. We still had hopes that mother and father were alive. And we got to Prague, Czechoslovakia. And lo and behold, a miracle did happen. We found our brother, Ben.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you find him?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was working for the owner as a driver and attached to the Russian Army. And he had an apartment. So he took us to his apartment and fed us. We got us some clothes. And more than anything else, he told us that another brother-- when he was liberated by the Russians and he joined the Russian forces, he saw our brother, Bernie. And a couple weeks later, Bernie shows up, with his girlfriend, who was nice-- wife, for over, over 50 years. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he told us our brother, Philip, was alive. He also was liberated by the Russians. They were in the Hungarian forced-labor camps.

speaker: interviewer
text: Philip was the oldest-- is the oldest?

speaker: interviewee
text: He's the oldest. And then we got word that he was getting-- going to get married. Because his girlfriend was liberated in Belsen-Bergen. And they are going to get married.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we went back to our village. And lo and behold, we were there, celebrating the wedding. By that time, we were resigned to the fact that our parents were not alive. But meanwhile, we found our sister. She came back from Belsen-Bergen. She was liberated there. How did you find her?

speaker: interviewee
text: She came to Prague, also. And the nice part about it is that we were the first family that six brothers and one sister were alive. Nobody believed it. Nobody could believe it. Because most of them, either all of them were killed, or maybe one, maybe two, were alive, some of them, even three. But no family had all six brothers alive. And I didn't even know till about 15 years ago that  our stepbrother died the day of the liberation of Belsen-Bergen of typhoid. And our sister found him. But anyway, we went back to Russia for the wedding. And everything was fine. We had a lot of joy. I met some of our former employees, who were like brothers. They all came that were alive. A lot of them were not alive. Very few of our friends from the village were alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: Had your house been taken over?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. No, but that-- at that time, it was taken over. But my brother took it back. And we had the wedding right in the home, in the back there. But at that time, the Russians decided they were going to take it away from Czechoslovakia. And they clamped the border down. So I had to escape from Russia. [LAUGHS] And, and lo and behold, my brother Ben, because of his connection, he had a good idea. And, and, and I was hiding under a Russian lady officer's skirt to get through the border. And they don't bathe. They're not exactly clean. It wasn't a pleasant smell. [LAUGHS] But I hid there for probably a day and a half till we got to Vienna, Austria. And--

speaker: interviewee
text: And she agreed to this.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. And I came back to Prague, Czechoslovakia. And by that time, they started taking orphans-- and we were considered orphans-- to England. The British Jewish people formed an orphanage there. They took our sister, Rosalyn. And of course, I had a job in Prague.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your job there?

speaker: interviewee
text: My job was making transformers with the [NON-ENGLISH] firm, very nice people. And I-- Mr. [NON-ENGLISH] was a very devout Catholic. And on Sundays-- Saturdays, many times, I worked in the nuns-- in the nunnery, helping over there. But the nuns were wonderful to me because I had a lot of wounds. I had a lot of sickness. As a matter of fact, something hit me where I passed out. And when I recovered, I started breaking out in boils, in--

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in Prague.

speaker: interviewee
text: --infections all over me. I couldn't sleep in a bed. They had to suspend me. They took me to the hospital. And I overheard the doctor tell the nurses, no use wasting penicillin on him. He's not going to make it. Even though I already put on about 20, 25, pounds. Because when I was liberated, I was only 60 pounds. I was only about 80, 85 pounds. But when I heard it-- that they're not going to give me the medication-- that night, I tore the bed sheet-- I was on the third floor-- lowered myself, and somehow escaped from there. Because they had me quarantined. They didn't know what it was. And I managed to get back to the apartment. And I found the lady that-- whose son was working with me. And she wanted to adopt me, a Czech lady.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: I called them Mom and Dad. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't remember right now. She was-- she was from from, Yugoslavia-- former Yugoslavia. And he was a Czech aristocrat, very nice people-- Dovahy. And they were very wonderful to me. In fact, my brother, Bill, was in a tuberculosis sanatorium. And they gave me bread to take him, and take him all kinds of things. And they were really wonderful, wonderful people. And it suddenly dawned on me that my sister is going to England alone. She's a little girl. She went through enough suffering. And I better go with her.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went down. And lo and behold, they took me to England. And when I arrived there, I immediately said, I gotta go to Scotland where my sister is. Oh, she had gone before you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. She went maybe three, four weeks, maybe a month, before me. And finally, they took me there. It was outside of Edinburgh, Scotland. And a lot of wonderful things had happened to me at that time to help me become a better man. For one thing, I started getting involved with sports. And it helped me become stronger and stronger. And because I tried so hard-- I, I managed to be involved in soccer and running. And I-- I won the blue ribbon.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the person that gave me the blue ribbon said, oh, my father is from the Continent. They called the Continent the rest of Europe. Why don't I pick you up some Sunday morning and have you come to our home for breakfast? And for a free breakfast, I'd go anyplace.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when he picked me up-- their name was Lebo. And they had the biggest meat-packing company in, in Scotland-- in Glasgow. And my sister was with me, and we had a great meal.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said, Lebo, where are you from? And we talked in Yiddish because I didn't know English, certainly not Scottish. [LAUGHS] And he says, I'm from Palanka. Turned out, he was a first cousin to my mother.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they were very nice to us. But the children might have thought-- I got the feeling the children thought that maybe their father was going to take us in. And so I said, no, I don't think that. I have too much to work to recover. I don't think any jealousy of-- anybody could look at me that way. And then they-- when they transferred us to Nelson, Lancashire, to another orphanage home-- and we went with a group. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: How many children were with you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Scotland, it must've been about 25, 30 kids. Then, in Burnley, there must've been about 60, 70 kids. But there was another orphanage home in Glasgow, a big one. And there was another big orphanage home in Manchester, England, which is close to Burnley, Lancashire. And as a matter of fact, the boys that were with me, I'm still in touch with them. We're still the dearest of friends. I saw one of them in Cincinnati two weeks ago. And I talked to the other one a couple of days ago in Los Angeles. He's still in the same business that-- when we went to London, England-- because I found out that our brother Sam was permitted to come to London-- we went there. And Jerry Hornstein was there before me. And he got a job with the George Nissel Company in contact lenses. In those days, they used to be made out of glass. They used to be the whole-- covering the whole eye. And the boss was a Hungarian Jew. And he loved Jerry. And when he met me, he says, you come and work for me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was Jerry a friend of yours from childhood, or someone you met at the orphanage?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, he was in-- from the orphanage, from Scotland and, and, and Lancashire, and then here. And we have stayed the dearest of friends since that time. As a matter of fact, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he and I became specialists. And we helped develop the corneal contact lens in England. He stayed in the business, and I got out of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What time would this be? Was it still 1945? Was it 1946?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is 1946 already. When we were in London, and I met and befriendlied-- first, my brother, Sam, met this family, the Rolf family. And he and I, and my sister, wanted to be on our own. And we got a teeny, teeny little room the three of us stayed in. And believe it or not, it was smaller than my closet. But somehow, we-- the three of us stayed in it. And it was a wonderful, wonderful experience. But it was very difficult to live there. But somehow we made it. And the Rolf family took a liking to us. And they let my sister and I move in there. And-- but my brother and I tossed up. He wanted to go to Israel. And I wanted to go to Israel. This is before Israel became a state. And he won, so he went to Israel. Now, unfortunately, he got injured very bad in the war of liberation. But the Rolf family were absolutely wonderful to us. I called them Mom and Dad Rolf. In fact, when they-- because I, I didn't stay there. And it was kind of a crazy, interesting story as to what happened there. But they were wonderful people, really. In fact, I am still in touch with the whole family.
